thesis: Pioneering the world's first space-based cellular broadband network enabling standard smartphones to connect directly to satellites without specialized hardware. First-mover advantage in direct-to-cell technology with 5 BlueBird satellites operational. Partnerships with AT&T, Verizon, Vodafone, and 50+ global carriers represent nearly 3 billion potential subscribers. $70.9M revenue in 2025 (up 1,505%) proves technology works, but extreme 447x P/S valuation prices in massive execution success. Heavy competition from SpaceX Starlink is the primary threat.

drivers: 45-60 satellites achieve continuous U.S. coverage by end of 2026
Verizon and AT&T commercial launches drive 5%+ rural adoption
International partnerships (Vodafone, Rakuten, stc) generate material revenue
Government contracts scale to $200M+ annually with high margins
SpaceX competition less severe than feared; carriers prefer ASTS neutral model

risks: SpaceX Starlink with T-Mobile captures 70%+ market share
AT&T or Verizon switch allegiance to SpaceX or develop in-house solutions
Satellite launches face delays or technical issues
Revenue growth stalls below $200M annually; cash burn forces dilutive financing
Market loses patience with 'story stocks'; multiple compression to 50x sales

base_case.assumptions: Successful satellite deployment with mixed adoption; SpaceX competition real but ASTS maintains 30-40% market share; Revenue grows to $400-600M by 2027; EBITDA breakeven by 2028-2029.

catalysts.event: Verizon Service Launch — Major carrier going live
Constellation Completion (45-60 satellites) — Continuous coverage milestone
Government Contract Wins — Higher-margin revenue validation

key_risks: SpaceX competition with deeper pockets and lower launch costs
Burning $1.2B+ annually with 2-year runway requiring future capital raises
Extreme 447x P/S valuation vulnerable to any execution miss
Customer concentration with AT&T and Verizon as key partners
Technology execution risk — satellites are complex with launch/manufacturing risks
